title: Meaningful links

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific HTML: Semantic and Accessible Web Documents draft for Meaningful links; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: Web Content Accessibility Guidelines 2.2; W3C Recommendation 2023; provisions applicable to Meaningful links
sources.claim: An anchor with href creates navigation, and its accessible name should describe the destination or action in context.

sources.locator: HTML Living Standard; living standard accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Meaningful links
sources.claim: An anchor with href creates navigation, and its accessible name should describe the destination or action in context.

canonicalExplanation.summary: An anchor with href creates navigation, and its accessible name should describe the destination or action in context.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A link named Read the C arrays lesson communicates its destination without surrounding visual context. Your goal is to improve a navigation set so destinations and current context are understandable. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Sections and landmarks; pf tools files; Relative and absolute URLs; Paragraph and inline meaning. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: An anchor with href creates navigation, and its accessible name should describe the destination or action in context. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Placeholder anchors, click-only spans, repeated vague names, and invalid nested interactive controls undermine keyboard and assistive use. This draft therefore rejects: Any element with a click handler is automatically equivalent to an anchor.

includes: Write link text that identifies purpose in and out of context and avoid fake-button anchors.
Improve a navigation set so destinations and current context are understandable.
excludes: Placeholder anchors, click-only spans, repeated vague names, and invalid nested interactive controls undermine keyboard and assistive use.

outcomes: Improve a navigation set so destinations and current context are understandable.

examples.title: Meaningful links: concrete case
examples.situation: A link named Read the C arrays lesson communicates its destination without surrounding visual context.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A link named Read the C arrays lesson communicates its destination without surrounding visual context.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Use an anchor with href for navigation and give it a destination-specific accessible name.
examples.result: The result follows because Use an anchor with href for navigation and give it a destination-specific accessible name.

examples.title: Meaningful links: boundary case
examples.situation: Placeholder anchors, click-only spans, repeated vague names, and invalid nested interactive controls undermine keyboard and assistive use.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: Placeholder anchors, click-only spans, repeated vague names, and invalid nested interactive controls undermine keyboard and assistive use.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: Any element with a click handler is automatically equivalent to an anchor.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Use an anchor with href for navigation and give it a destination-specific accessible name.
